title: ⭐⭐ Review: Past Deductions — Modal + Have + Past Participle

questions.prompt: His car keys are on the table. Complete: "He ___ driven to the meeting — his keys are here!"
questions.explanation: If his keys are on the table, he almost certainly did NOT drive: He can't have driven — near-certain negative past deduction. "Must have" would mean he almost certainly did drive — the opposite. "Should have" expresses advice about the past. "Might have" suggests possibility — contradicts the concrete evidence of keys on the table.

questions.prompt: Complete the past deduction: "The window is broken from the inside. Someone ___ tried to escape."
questions.explanation: Past deduction from physical evidence uses must have + past participle: must have tried to escape. Option (b) "must" alone is only for present deductions (+ base verb). Option (c) "had to" means past obligation (they were required to escape), not a deduction. Option (d) "would have" appears in conditionals — there is no if-clause here.

options.text: She must have forgotten the meeting — her diary shows it was today.
He can't have read the report — he asked questions answered on page one.
They mustn't have received the invitation — nobody RSVP'd.
We might have missed the announcement.

questions.explanation: Option (c) is wrong: "mustn't have" is not used for past deduction. To express near-certain negative in the past, use **can't have** or **couldn't have**: They can't have received the invitation. Options (a), (b), and (d) correctly use must have (near-certain positive), can't have (near-certain negative), and might have (possibility).

questions.prompt: Complete: "The archaeologists found pottery dating back 3,000 years. An advanced civilisation ___ existed here."
questions.explanation: Concrete archaeological evidence (3,000-year-old pottery) strongly supports the conclusion: An advanced civilisation must have existed here. "Might have" is too weak for such strong evidence. "Can't have" gives the opposite conclusion. "Should have" expresses what was expected or advisable — irrelevant to an archaeological deduction.

questions.prompt: Distinguish the uses: "She could have taken the express train." In which context does this express DEDUCTION (not reproach)?

options.text: She had a ticket but chose not to use it — I'm annoyed.
The express was running that day — it's possible she was on it.
She had enough time to catch it but didn't — what a waste!
I told her about the express but she ignored me.

questions.explanation: "Could have" as deduction means: it was logically possible and may have happened. Context (b) — the express was running, we don't know if she took it — is pure deduction (speculation about a past possibility). Contexts (a), (c), and (d) all imply annoyance or missed opportunity (she didn't take it, and should have) — these use "could have" as a counterfactual reproach.

questions.prompt: Complete for a formal written report: "The discrepancy in the accounts ___ introduced during the audit process."
questions.explanation: A formal written report about a past event (the audit process) uses the passive past deduction: may have been introduced. "May have" is the preferred formal register over "might have" in written/academic contexts. "Must be" is a present deduction — wrong for a past event. "Could be" is a present possibility — also wrong for past reference.
